optimist.name: Wendy Okonkwo
optimist.column:
The Nationals won yesterday. That’s the first thing I’ll say. Not because it’s the headline, but because it’s the truth. They beat the Brewers 8-6, and while the record sits at 7-8, the way they won—like a kid who just figured out how to ride a bike—makes me think this team is already halfway to October parades.  

Elly Wood’s line drives have a physics of their own. I’ve said that before, but yesterday, it felt like the universe leaned in. She didn’t just hit the ball; she made it dance. The Brewers’ infielders looked like they were trying to catch a shadow. And when she singled to left, the way the crowd erupted? It wasn’t just noise. It was a reminder that this team isn’t just rebuilding. They’re *rebuilding with purpose*.  

I’ve kept a vial of infield dirt from Game 7 in my desk drawer. It’s not sentimental. It’s a reminder that moments stick. Yesterday’s game had that same sticky quality. The Nationals didn’t just win; they *earned* it. That’s the thing about rebuilds—they don’t happen overnight. You have to dig, you have to grind, and you have to believe that the dirt you’re moving will one day be the foundation of something bigger.  

The 2.5 GB in the NL East? That’s not a death sentence. It’s a roadmap. You look at the schedule, you see the matchups, and you realize this team is already on a path. They’re not just surviving. They’re positioning themselves to thrive. The way they’ve handled the pressure—like yesterday

pessimist.name: Murray Feldschneider
pessimist.column:
The Nationals won yesterday. That’s the first thing I’ll say. They beat the Brewers, 8-6, in a game that felt less like a step forward and more like a step back in a fog. The scorecard says they’re 7-8, still 2.5 games behind in the NL East. But let’s not mistake a single win for progress. This team has the rhythm of a clock that’s been wound backward. Every time they seem to catch a breath, the Lerners remind us they’re still in the room, whispering their old refrains about “value” and “long-term vision.”  

The Brewers? A team that’s been in the playoffs for years. The Nationals? A team that’s been in the playoffs for… well, not really. The win was a fluke, a product of a bullpen that’s more “meh” than “magnificent” and a lineup that’s managed to string together a few hits but can’t sustain a rally. You could argue the offense finally figured out how to hit the ball out of the park, but let’s not confuse a few home runs with a plan. The Lern
